jokes.text: Ilana describing a long butt hair that turned out to be head hair fallen into her butt crack
jokes.setup: Ilana opens mid-thought about a concerning discovery regarding her body
jokes.punchline: The relief/disgust pivot: it's not a butt hair, it's her head hair fallen into her crack — as if that's better
jokes.explanation: Perfect cold open energy for Broad City — in medias res gross-out that only Ilana would say out loud AND find comforting. The 'yeah, duh!' Abbi response treats it as totally normal, which doubles the joke. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: 'You are finally going to vagina-swallow Jeremy tonight'
jokes.setup: Transition from butt-hair conversation to Abbi's upcoming date
jokes.punchline: 'Vagina-swallow' as a euphemism for having sex — a made-up compound verb delivered with total confidence
jokes.explanation: This is peak Ilana Glazer verbal invention — a brand-new sex euphemism that's simultaneously vivid, absurd, and anatomically creative. Nobody else writes or delivers a line like this. The economy is exceptional: three words do what a paragraph couldn't. Scores 8+ because it's genuinely novel coinage with perfect character specificity.

jokes.text: Ilana's confident assertions about Jeremy: 'He definitely has sisters' / 'He also definitely doesn't wear underwear. He's got his junk rolling around in there all smelly and stuff.'
jokes.setup: Abbi says she wants to know basic facts about Jeremy
jokes.punchline: Ilana's 'definitely' pronouncements escalate from plausible (sisters) to specific sensory hallucination (free-balling junk smell)
jokes.explanation: The escalation from 'sisters' to unprompted free-balling smell assessment is classic Ilana — she has parasocial sexual intel on Abbi's crush. The confidence of 'definitely' on both claims is the engine of the joke.

jokes.text: This bitch is always late. Oh, here she is. I can't believe you beat me here! This bitch is always late. - Hi, mom. - Hi, honey.
jokes.setup: Ilana complains 'this bitch is always late' — audience assumes she's talking about a friend
jokes.punchline: The 'bitch' is her own mother, who then uses the exact same phrase about Ilana — establishing them as mirrors of each other
jokes.explanation: Justifies 8+: The structure here is nearly perfect. Setup lands the misdirection (we think she's talking about a peer), reveal lands the warmth/shock (it's her mom), and the instant mirror — mom says the exact same line back — is an elegant economy move that tells us everything about this mother-daughter dynamic in one beat. The callback within seconds makes it a doubled joke. Genuinely well-constructed.

jokes.text: Ilana says 'This bitch is always late' — then Bobbi arrives and Ilana says it again: 'This bitch is always late'
jokes.setup: Ilana complains they didn't need to rush because the person is always late
jokes.punchline: The exact same line is repeated beat-for-beat after Bobbi shows up — Ilana doesn't skip a beat
jokes.explanation: Near-perfect micro-structure: first line reads as observation, second line is identical but now said TO the person's face with zero self-awareness. The brevity and exact repetition is the craft. Ilana calling Bobbi 'this bitch' affectionately to her face is also character-specific warmth-through-rudeness.

jokes.text: Bobbi says 'Yelp said somebody got staph infection in this place. It's so cheap now.' — as if staph is a positive endorsement
jokes.setup: The group arrives at the nail salon
jokes.punchline: Bobbi treats a staph infection Yelp review as proof the salon is a bargain, not a warning
jokes.explanation: Excellent character introduction for Bobbi — her entire worldview encoded in one line. The 'it's so cheap now' pivot recontextualizes the staph report as deal-hunting logic. Pure character comedy that's instantly quotable.

jokes.text: 'Staph is not the real bad one, right?' / 'Yeah, it is.'
jokes.setup: Ilana tries to rationalize going to the staph-infected salon
jokes.punchline: Abbi's flat one-beat confirmation — yes it is, no further discussion
jokes.explanation: Short, economical straight-man beat. Abbi's deadpan 'Yeah, it is' after Ilana's wishful thinking is well-timed. Not exceptional but efficient.

jokes.text: Ilana describes Abbi to her own mother as 'a high-class wasp-y Jew. A Philadelphia queen from down the main line, king of Prussia mall!'
jokes.setup: Bobbi asks why Abbi doesn't bring her own nail polish for touch-ups
jokes.punchline: Ilana delivers an increasingly grandiose, contradictory social-class bio of Abbi that climaxes at 'King of Prussia mall'
jokes.explanation: The oxymoron of 'WASP-y Jew' followed by the weirdly proud 'King of Prussia mall' anticlimax is funny — Ilana is both roasting and defending Abbi simultaneously. Very specific suburban Philadelphia reference gives it texture.

jokes.text: Visual gag: nail polish bottles slapping in unison — presumably Bobbi and Ilana doing some kind of synchronized nail polish display
jokes.setup: Ilana has just introduced Abbi in grandiose terms to Bobbi
jokes.punchline: Mother and daughter perform an unannounced synchronized bottle-slap ritual in the nail salon
jokes.explanation: The sound cue implies a shared mother-daughter physical ritual — the fact that it exists at all, and in a nail salon, is the joke. Rewatch value because you'd want to see the actual choreography.

jokes.text: Bobbi's eulogy for Grandma Esther: 'She played for the Rockford Peaches. She sang at the Cotton Club. And she fucked Little Richard!'
jokes.setup: Bobbi tells Abbi not to cry for Grandma Esther because she lived 92 full years
jokes.punchline: Three biographical achievements escalate from impressive (Rockford Peaches) to legendary (Cotton Club) to completely unverifiable and transgressive (fucked Little Richard) — delivered as equal accomplishments
jokes.explanation: One of the best jokes in the episode. The Rule of Three is executed perfectly: two historically grounded achievements (A League of Their Own reference, Harlem Renaissance venue) then a detonation. The flat equivalence of sexual history with cultural achievement is the engine. Bobbi's delivery treats all three as equally creditable. Also does thematic work — this establishes Grandma Esther's 'lived with no regrets' legend that motivates Abbi later. Scores 8+ because the structure, specificity, and escalation are all doing heavy lifting simultaneously.

jokes.text: 'Wait, I thought Little Richard was gay.' / 'Bisexual alien.' / 'No, bisexual alien.'
jokes.setup: Abbi raises the logical objection to Grandma Esther's Little Richard encounter
jokes.punchline: Ilana and Bobbi simultaneously correct her to 'bisexual alien' — as if this is established fact, and the alien part goes completely unquestioned
jokes.explanation: The episode's single best joke. The setup expects a correction to 'bisexual' — reasonable, defensible. But 'alien' is added as a completely matter-of-fact modifier, and the mother-daughter synchrony ('No, bisexual alien' / 'Bisexual alien') treats it as shared family knowledge. The joke doesn't explain itself, doesn't wink. Economy is near-perfect. The 'No,' before repeating the same thing is a beautiful rhythm choice. This is exceptional television comedy writing — small, dense, absurdist, character-specific, and quotable forever.

jokes.text: Bobbi: 'Gets me a chance to go to Canal Street and pick up some handbags for the girls back on Long Island.' Ilana: 'Mom, I thought we were done with the bag obsession.' Bobbi: 'Excuse me, Ilana, my mother just died. I was done, but now I'm dipping my toe back in.'
jokes.setup: They're at a nail salon mourning Grandma Esther
jokes.punchline: Bobbi uses her mother's death as emotional justification to restart a counterfeit handbag habit — 'I was done, but now I'm dipping my toe back in' as grief response
jokes.explanation: Scores 8 because 'dipping my toe back in' as grief language applied to counterfeit bag addiction is genuinely sharp. Bobbi's character logic is internally consistent — this IS her coping mechanism — and the straight-faced invocation of bereavement to justify it is dark comedy done right.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'One time, I don't wear a condom and you tell your...' / Abbi: 'No! That's just how she says goodbye to people.'
jokes.setup: Abbi tries to defend Bobbi's condom comment
jokes.punchline: Ilana's protest implies Bobbi only says it because of a specific incident Ilana caused — Abbi's insistent denial can't erase the implication
jokes.explanation: Good callback that deepens joke 14. The interrupted sentence implies a whole backstory, and Abbi's forceful denial-as-acceptance is funny. The 'yeah, I care' Bobbi adds from offscreen is a nice final button.

jokes.text: Ilana and Bobbi admiring Abbi's butt as she walks away — Abbi: 'Talking about my butt?' Both: 'Yes.'
jokes.setup: Abbi is walking away; Ilana and Bobbi comment admiringly on 'that gorgeous tush'
jokes.punchline: Abbi turns and hopefully asks if they're talking about her butt. Immediate unified 'Yes.' — no hesitation, no shame
jokes.explanation: Mother-daughter synchronized objectification of Abbi is sweet and funny simultaneously. The synchrony echoes the 'bisexual alien' double-response beat, which gives it structural resonance. Abbi's hope in the question is very her.

jokes.text: Ilana and Bobbi casually discuss Jay-Z and Beyoncé being in the Illuminati, and Blue Ivy becoming 'grandmaster supreme'
jokes.setup: Mother and daughter walking to Canal Street mid-conversation
jokes.punchline: They treat Illuminati theory as settled fact, but the specificity of 'grandmaster supreme' for Blue Ivy is the escalation that sells it
jokes.explanation: Fun ambient comedy that establishes the Wexler family's shared conspiratorial worldview. 'Lucky kid' as Bobbi's sincere reaction is good. Not exceptional but textural.

jokes.text: On-screen title card: 'Buying counterfeit bags is illegal.'
jokes.setup: The women are excitedly heading to buy knockoffs
jokes.punchline: The show breaks the fourth wall with a dry legal disclaimer, treating its own premise as a PSA
jokes.explanation: The show naming the episode 'Knockoffs' and then flashing this disclaimer is structurally elegant self-awareness. The deadpan timing — right as they arrive — works like a legal warning in a movie about heists. Economy score is 10 because four words do maximum comedic work.

jokes.text: Ilana speaks Mandarin to the vendor then immediately: 'What the fuck am I talking Chinese for?'
jokes.setup: Ilana is attempting to navigate the Canal Street counterfeit market
jokes.punchline: She speaks actual Mandarin then snaps herself out of it with total self-bewilderment
jokes.explanation: The surprise that Ilana apparently speaks some Mandarin, followed by her own confusion about why she's using it, is perfectly Ilana. The line is completely honest about its absurdity. Character integration is high — only Ilana would do this and only Ilana would wonder why she's doing it.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'His lips are his hottest feature!' / Bobbi: 'Not for me.'
jokes.setup: Bobbi criticizes Jay-Z's lips during the rushed bag operation
jokes.punchline: Ilana defends the lips; Bobbi's 'Not for me' delivered as firm personal aesthetic judgment mid-sprint
jokes.explanation: The deadpan firmness of 'Not for me' as a sexual preference declaration during a criminal chase is good economy comedy.

jokes.text: The group is offered blindfolds for the covert bag operation; Ilana and Bobbi pull out their own: 'We bring our own blindfolds, no. Thank you.'
jokes.setup: They're being led deeper into the counterfeit operation and offered vendor blindfolds
jokes.punchline: Mother and daughter have come prepared with personal blindfolds, declining the communal ones — the implication being they've done this many times
jokes.explanation: Scores 8+ because this is dense with implications: they are repeat customers, they've thought about hygiene in this criminal context, and the refusal of communal blindfolds (while cheerfully accepting the criminal enterprise) is perfect comic logic. The pink eye callback adds another layer. Character integration is high — this is exactly the Wexler family's specific type of cluelessness about proportionality.

jokes.text: 'Remember that time I got pink eye? That was so horrible.'
jokes.setup: They've just refused the vendor's blindfolds on hygiene grounds
jokes.punchline: Ilana's pink eye reminiscence confirms this has happened before — the covert Canal Street bag run is a recurring family tradition
jokes.explanation: The callback confirms the backstory implied by the personal blindfolds. Good button on the bit.

jokes.text: Jeremy reveals his apartment has no walls ('I knocked down all the walls. Hate walls.') in response to Abbi's compliment
jokes.setup: Abbi compliments Jeremy's apartment
jokes.punchline: Jeremy explains he removed all walls — 'Hate walls' — as a simple, non-negotiable design philosophy
jokes.explanation: 'Hate walls' is a perfect three-word character sketch of a certain type of bougie NYC creative. The economy is excellent and it sets up the later joke about Abbi judging him based on his stuff — his aesthetic is established here.

jokes.text: Jeremy: 'I collected them on the last road trip I took with my terminal dog... miss you, Betsy.'
jokes.setup: Abbi admires Jeremy's postcard collection
jokes.punchline: 'Terminal dog' dropped mid-sentence as a casual modifier, followed by a sincere '...miss you, Betsy' — the grief is real but the syntax is absurd
jokes.explanation: The phrase 'terminal dog' as a casual adjective is darkly economical and very funny. The sentiment is real but the framing is absurd. It's also a setup for the 'surrounded by death' Abbi beat and the 'Six Feet Under' bit.

jokes.text: Abbi, clearly about to say something profound: 'And sometimes death really makes me think about...' long pause '...Life.'
jokes.setup: Abbi is connecting with Jeremy over recent deaths; builds a contemplative pause suggesting deep wisdom
jokes.punchline: 'Life.' — the most obvious possible completion, delivered as revelation
jokes.explanation: The extended pause before 'Life' is the entire joke — the audience expects Abbi to say something profound and she delivers a truism. The sincerity with which both characters then treat it as a deep insight adds a second layer. 'To life. And to remember, and to being. And... and... that's it.' is a great trailing-off non-conclusion.

jokes.text: Jeremy: 'My favorite TV show is Six Feet Under, so that's probably why.' / Abbi: 'You haven't seen Six Feet Under?!' / Jeremy: 'Oh, God. You're so lucky.'
jokes.setup: Jeremy says he's eloquent about death because he loves Six Feet Under, asks if Abbi's seen it
jokes.punchline: When Abbi hasn't seen it, Jeremy doesn't say 'you should watch it' — instead: 'You're so lucky.' The reversal of the expected 'watch this show' pitch
jokes.explanation: The subversion is clean: we expect the 'you HAVE to watch it' recommendation; instead Jeremy envies her the ability to experience it for the first time. Both funny and character-revealing — Jeremy is exactly this kind of guy. 'You're so lucky' lands well.

jokes.text: Jeremy wipes something off Abbi's face as a prelude to their first kiss — the camera cuts to Abbi's internal monologue: 'Oh my God, I'm kissing Jeremy.'
jokes.setup: The romantic moment builds with Jeremy offering his box set
jokes.punchline: Abbi's internal monologue is pure giddy chaos — she's narrating it to herself in the moment
jokes.explanation: The parallel internal monologues (Abbi/Jeremy) are a sweet structural joke. Functional romantic comedy beat with good character specificity for Abbi's anxious inner life.

jokes.text: Cut to Jeremy's internal monologue: 'Oh my God, I'm kissing Abbi.' — beat-for-beat parallel
jokes.setup: Abbi's giddy internal monologue (id 31)
jokes.punchline: Jeremy has the exact same internal monologue — the joke is that they're both equally stunned, equally dorky
jokes.explanation: The mirror structure is the joke — it's a mutual dorkiness reveal. Works well as a comedic button on the romantic scene.

jokes.text: Ilana on copepods: 'We have shrimp inside of us at all times, which I'm okay with, sounds delicious. But it's like, ask me first?'
jokes.setup: Ilana has revealed NYC drinking water contains copepods
jokes.punchline: Her objection isn't health — it's consent. She would have said yes to internal shrimp if asked.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's top three jokes. Ilana's framing of involuntary internal shrimp as a consent violation rather than a health issue is genuinely original and perfectly character-integrated. Nobody else could write or deliver this. The 'sounds delicious' before the consent complaint is the exact right absurdist pivot. Scores 8+ because this is the kind of line that gets quoted for years — it captures Ilana's specific blend of body-positive hedonism, wellness culture self-awareness, and comic non-sequitur.

jokes.text: 'Now we're cooking with gas. All the good shit is always down a manhole.'
jokes.setup: They've been led underground through a manhole to access the secret counterfeit bag warehouse
jokes.punchline: Ilana treats descending into a manhole for fake bags as a general life principle
jokes.explanation: 'All the good shit is always down a manhole' is quotable life philosophy delivered without irony. The idiom upgrade from 'underground' to literal manhole works well.

jokes.text: Jeremy: 'Do you want to maybe switch? Mix it up a little?' — and then produces a strap-on
jokes.setup: 'Switch' implies switching positions — a totally normal sexual suggestion
jokes.punchline: Jeremy meant switch who's doing the pegging — he hands Abbi a strap-on
jokes.explanation: The misdirection is well-structured — 'switch' is entirely plausible as position-switching, and the delivery presumably keeps it ambiguous until the visual reveal. The whole episode's B-plot hinges on this pivot. Scores high on impact because it's the episode's central comedic surprise.

jokes.text: (Whispering) 'It's a shinjo.'
jokes.setup: Abbi has just been handed a strap-on and is processing the situation
jokes.punchline: Her only murmured response is the brand identification — she recognizes the model of the dildo
jokes.explanation: Three words, whispered, that do enormous work. Abbi's priority in this unprecedented moment isn't panic or negotiation — it's product identification. The specificity of 'shinjo' as a recognizable brand in her world (and its later thematic importance to the episode's knockoff motif) is exceptional. The whisper delivery sells it completely. This is the kind of joke that only works in this show for these characters — it's pure Abbi Jacobson. The economy score is 10.

jokes.text: 'Right in the butt.'
jokes.setup: Abbi has just identified the shinjo and is processing what Jeremy wants
jokes.punchline: She summarizes the entire situation in four words, delivered flatly
jokes.explanation: Maximum economy — four words recapping an entire sexual situation with perfect comic timing. The flatness of the delivery (we infer this from context) is everything. Scores 8+ because there's no wasted syllable and the understatement carries enormous comedic weight.

jokes.text: Abbi misunderstands Jeremy's 'switch' as suggesting they swap partners — 'Oh, you just meant switch positions?'
jokes.setup: Jeremy hands her a strap-on; she uses it. Then he suggests they 'switch'
jokes.punchline: Abbi thought 'switch' meant swap partners (with whom?), creating layered confusion — Jeremy meant switch who's wearing the strap-on, Abbi heard couple-swapping
jokes.explanation: Good cringe setup — the misunderstanding layers on the already unusual situation. 'I'm just getting, you know, the feel' is a nice awkward recovery.

jokes.text: Ilana fielding calls from Bobbi's address book while getting a manicure: 'Rachel Ackerman, Rachel Alterman, Aunt Cherry, Rabbi Stein...' then her phone rings: 'Cut or uncut?'
jokes.setup: Ilana is making Shiva calls on behalf of Bobbi from a list of names
jokes.punchline: When her own phone rings, she answers it with 'Cut or uncut?' — the question is about penis (because Abbi is presumably calling about Jeremy), but it sounds like it could be a rabbi question or a deli order
jokes.explanation: The list of Jewish names (including 'Aunt Cherry' and 'Rabbi Stein') sets a very specific cultural context, then 'Cut or uncut?' lands perfectly — it sounds like a Jewish circumcision question in context but she immediately pivots to penis assessment. Multivalent joke working on multiple levels simultaneously. Scores 8 for the double meaning and the cultural specificity of the setup.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'Dude, I'm freaking the fuck out right now. So we were doing it and I was like, we should switch positions, and then he throws me a strap-on.' [BEAT]
jokes.setup: Abbi calls Ilana mid-crisis for advice
jokes.punchline: The 'Hold, please.' — Ilana puts Abbi on hold to absorb this information
jokes.explanation: 'Hold, please' is the right reaction — it's professional phone etiquette applied to a sexual emergency. The scene cut that presumably follows (to Ilana's face processing) would be the visual kicker.

jokes.text: Ilana answers the other line while Abbi is on hold: 'Hello? I get one, too. And I think the rest are just gonna be extras, so yeah.'
jokes.setup: Ilana put Abbi on hold to take another call
jokes.punchline: The other call is apparently also about the strap-on situation — Ilana is already acquiring her own and planning inventory
jokes.explanation: The implication that Ilana has a separate supply chain for strap-ons already in motion while Abbi is on crisis hold is exceptional character comedy. 'I think the rest are just gonna be extras' — extras — is the peak Ilana detail. Scores 8+ because the joke reveals character (she's been waiting for this), creates stakes, and is delivered with zero explanation.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'This is a dream come true. Thank you for sharing this with me.' / Abbi: 'Dude, I'm calling for advice.' / Ilana: 'Oh. Sorry.'
jokes.setup: Abbi is in crisis; Ilana is treating it as her personal windfall
jokes.punchline: When reminded this is a call for advice, Ilana recalibrates — 'Oh. Sorry.' — as if she'd forgotten Abbi was in distress
jokes.explanation: Ilana's pure vicarious joy followed by the polite recalibration is the Ilana/Abbi dynamic at its best. The 'Oh. Sorry.' as a beat of genuine reconsideration is perfectly timed.

jokes.text: 'All throughout college, I slept with a strap-on on, just in case the opportunity came along that you have handed to you on a silver fucking platter!'
jokes.setup: Ilana is trying to convince Abbi to peg Jeremy
jokes.punchline: The revelation that Ilana wore a strap-on to bed for four years of college 'just in case' — preposterous commitment to preparedness
jokes.explanation: Scores 8+ because this is a genuinely outrageous and perfectly specific character revelation. The logic is Ilana's own: scout's honor preparedness applied to pegging. 'All throughout college' implies four years of nightly strap-on readiness. The 'silver fucking platter' elevates it further. This is quotable, specific, impossible to imagine coming from any other character, and structurally perfect. One of the episode's standout jokes.

jokes.text: At Shiva: Ilana delivers funeral-context argument for pegging: 'That badass bitch did everything she ever wanted to. You want to go to the grave dreaming of Jeremy's hairy, adorable little butthole? Or do you want to die knowing that you brought him pleasure by plowing it like a queen?'
jokes.setup: They're at Grandma Esther's Shiva; Ilana needs to convince Abbi to peg Jeremy
jokes.punchline: Ilana frames pegging Jeremy as a tribute to Grandma Esther's legacy of living fully — 'Jeremy's hairy, adorable little butthole' said at a Jewish funeral
jokes.explanation: Scores 8+ because the joke does everything at once: it pays off the Grandma Esther setup from the first act, it's a callback to the 'live with no regrets' theme, it's delivered at the most inappropriate possible moment, and 'hairy, adorable little butthole' is both gross and strangely tender. The logic is airtight within Ilana's worldview: Grandma Esther fucked Little Richard, therefore Abbi must peg Jeremy. Philosophically consistent comedy.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'Bitch, you know. You wouldn't have called me if you didn't.'
jokes.setup: Abbi says she doesn't know if she should do it
jokes.punchline: Ilana cuts through with dead-on insight — the call itself is the answer
jokes.explanation: Short, precise, and emotionally true — which makes it funnier. Ilana is a better friend when she's being confidently direct than when she's being absurd. The economy here is excellent.

jokes.text: Jeremy: 'I volunteered to teach some underprivileged kids how to do some woodworking today. I'm running a little bit late.'
jokes.setup: Jeremy finds Abbi still in his apartment after a night of pegging
jokes.punchline: Jeremy is a guy who pegs on the first date AND volunteers to teach woodworking to underprivileged kids — the combination creates an absurdly perfect human being
jokes.explanation: The contrast between the sexual transgression of the night and Jeremy's wholesome morning errand is perfectly observed. 'Wow, that's beautiful' is Abbi's reaction to being told she's being left for underprivileged woodworking — correctly identifying this makes him MORE attractive.

jokes.text: Jeremy: 'Listen, stay as long as you want. Have a great day.' [door closes] 'Whoa. I mean, till tonight.'
jokes.setup: Jeremy says an open-ended 'stay as long as you want' as he leaves
jokes.punchline: Immediately corrects himself — 'I mean, till tonight' — he actually wants her gone by evening
jokes.explanation: The self-correction after the door closes (or as he's closing it) is good timing. The charming-guy-with-limits portrait is economically drawn.

jokes.text: Elliott (Ilana's dad) at the Shiva: 'My dad didn't love me half as much as I love you guys.' / Family: 'Okay.' / 'We know.'
jokes.setup: Emotional family reunion at the Shiva
jokes.punchline: 'We know' delivered without comfort — a family that has absorbed Elliott's emotional oversharing as ambient background noise
jokes.explanation: The matter-of-fact 'We know' to his emotional revelation is perfect — this family has evolved beyond responding to Elliott's need for validation. The 'Okay' before it is even funnier as a non-hug.

jokes.text: Elliott: 'And the toll into the city? 13 bucks. Now, you know that's going right into Bloomberg's pocket.' / Ilana: 'Bloomberg isn't mayor anymore.' / Elliott: 'That's what he wants you to think!'
jokes.setup: Elliott pivots from genuine emotional warmth to conspiracy theory
jokes.punchline: 'That's what he wants you to think' — Bloomberg has apparently engineered his own political disappearance to continue secretly collecting tolls
jokes.explanation: The Bloomberg conspiracy theory is a great piece of New York-specific absurdism. The structure is tight: factual correction → conspiracy pivot. 'That's what he wants you to think' is a perfect paranoid punchline. The Wexler family's shared conspiratorial worldview (cf. Illuminati, Fukushima) is consistent character work.

jokes.text: Scene: a woman in a sex shop complaining to the clerk: 'Once again, these nipple clamps are not tight enough. They keep popping off.' / Clerk: 'Well, I'm sorry, ma'am. That's as tight as they get.'
jokes.setup: Cut to a sex shop scene as Abbi enters
jokes.punchline: An older woman is returning nipple clamps as defective, with consumer-product complaint etiquette applied to sex toys
jokes.explanation: The application of Yelp-review customer complaint register to nipple clamp functionality is good. 'That's as tight as they get' as the clerk's resigned professional response is funny. The juxtaposition with Abbi's errand is also good scene structure.

jokes.text: Sex shop clerk: 'You tried to wash a dildo in a dishwasher?' / Abbi: 'I did indeed.'
jokes.setup: Abbi approaches the counter having melted a dildo in her dishwasher
jokes.punchline: The clerk sees through her fumbling immediately; 'I did indeed' is the most formal possible admission
jokes.explanation: 'I did indeed' is the right register — formal, dignified, like admitting to a minor traffic violation. The clerk's immediate correct diagnosis is also good. 'I don't think you're here for a staring contest' is a great dry line.

jokes.text: Clerk: 'I don't think you're here for a staring contest. Can I see it?'
jokes.setup: Abbi is fumbling with how to explain her situation
jokes.punchline: The clerk cuts through her embarrassment with professional directness — 'I don't think you're here for a staring contest'
jokes.explanation: 'I don't think you're here for a staring contest' is a great line — it's the voice of the unflappable sex shop professional who has seen everything. The economy is excellent and the implication (that she expected this exact type of customer) is funny.

jokes.text: Clerk examines the melted dildo: 'Mm-hmm. Parabolic tearing. Low curvature. All right, let's get to work.'
jokes.setup: Clerk examines the dishwasher-damaged dildo with forensic seriousness
jokes.punchline: Technical repair language applied to a sex toy — 'parabolic tearing, low curvature' as if this were an iPhone screen crack assessment
jokes.explanation: Scores 8+ because 'parabolic tearing' is the exact right level of technical specificity — real-sounding but absurd in context. The matter-of-fact 'let's get to work' treating dildo repair as a standard professional service is excellent. The clerk character is doing genuine comedic heavy lifting with complete dignity.

jokes.text: Clerk: 'You know, Shelly Winters purchased one of these once.'
jokes.setup: Clerk is repairing the dildo; making small talk
jokes.punchline: Celebrity dildo provenance — Shelly Winters, specifically — dropped as conversational anecdote
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'Shelly Winters' (not a glamorous celebrity choice — a deliberate comedic selection of someone unexpected) is the joke. Also connects thematically to the episode's Jewish cultural texture. The clerk's casual delivery of sex toy celebrity trivia is good character work.

jokes.text: Elliott is singing 'On Eagle's Wings' at the Shiva — Ilana asks: 'Isn't that a Christian song?'
jokes.setup: Elliott is leading music at the Jewish Shiva
jokes.punchline: He's chosen a song traditionally associated with Catholic masses — Ilana notes the religious mismatch
jokes.explanation: Good ambient character detail — Elliott is the kind of guy who'd sing a Christian song at a Jewish funeral without noticing. Functional but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Bobbi's friend farts loudly on opening the door to greet Abbi — 'Whoops.' / 'That'll be our little secret, okay?'
jokes.setup: Abbi arrives at the Shiva; the door opens warmly
jokes.punchline: Immediate loud fart, followed by an intimate secret-pact offer with someone she's just met
jokes.explanation: Functional gross-out gag elevated by the 'our little secret' social contract offer. Not exceptional — fart gags are cheap — but the post-fart negotiation is a nice touch.

jokes.text: Simply yes or no. Did you or did you not... (exhaling sharply) Peg? / (Elliott continues singing)
jokes.setup: Bobbi pulls Ilana aside at the Shiva for a formal inquiry
jokes.punchline: The formal 'simply yes or no' legal register is wildly incongruous with what she's asking; 'Peg?' delivered with an exhale is perfectly Bobbi — she can't quite say it normally either
jokes.explanation: Justifies 8+: The word economy here is exceptional. 'Simply yes or no' establishes a mock-judicial formality, the exhale before 'Peg?' humanizes Bobbi (even she finds it hard to say plainly), and the song continuing in the background is perfect cringe counterpoint. Bobbi treating pegging intel like sworn testimony is a great character beat.

jokes.text: 'Whoo, you a pegga! Pega-Seuss! That's you.'
jokes.setup: Ilana has just confirmed Abbi pegged Jeremy
jokes.punchline: 'Pega-Seuss' — a pun combining 'pegging' with Dr. Seuss — delivered at a Jewish funeral
jokes.explanation: 'Pega-Seuss' is a genuinely inspired portmanteau — combining the act with a beloved children's author in a way that's both absurd and weirdly apt (Dr. Seuss's invented words, the playful logic). It's quotable, original, and only Ilana would coin this. Scores 8+ for genuine originality in wordplay.

jokes.text: Bobbi inspects Abbi's bag at the Shiva: 'Ooh, this is a nice bag. Italian leather? ... Let me see... I'll be the judge of that.' / Abbi deflects; Bobbi: 'Abbi Anna Abrams! My mother's freshly dead! Now let me look at this lining!'
jokes.setup: Bobbi has been preoccupied with handbags all episode; Abbi is carrying a Canal Street knockoff
jokes.punchline: Bobbi invokes her freshly dead mother to demand access to inspect the bag's lining — grief weaponized for handbag authentication
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback and escalation — Bobbi used her mother's death earlier to justify going to Canal Street, and now uses it again to demand bag authentication access at the actual funeral. The full name ('Abbi Anna Abrams!') adds formality to an insane demand. 'Freshly dead' is specifically funny. Scores 8+ because this is character comedy with structural integrity — Bobbi's worldview is entirely consistent and the episode has earned this payoff.

jokes.text: Abbi Anna Abrams! My mother's freshly dead! Now let me look at this lining!
jokes.setup: Abbi is trying to prevent Bobbi from examining the knockoff bag
jokes.punchline: 'My mother's freshly dead!' invoked as entitlement to examine a handbag lining — the escalation from grief to consumer demand in one breath is peak Bobbi
jokes.explanation: Justifies 8+: This is one of the episode's best lines. 'My mother's freshly dead!' as a logical premise for bag-lining inspection rights is a brilliant non-sequitur that's also completely coherent within Bobbi's character logic. The word 'freshly' is doing enormous comic work — it's precise and slightly grotesque, like a cooking term applied to death. The full-name invocation ('Abbi Anna Abrams') establishes the mock-seriousness. Deep character integration: Bobbi's grief and consumerism are inseparable.

jokes.text: Ooh, waterproof, love that. Uh-huh. Nice backstitch. You really are a high-class jewess, let me tell ya.
jokes.setup: Bobbi finally gets to inspect the bag she thinks is Italian leather
jokes.punchline: Her expert approval — specific technical praise ('backstitch') — is a perfect irony because the bag is a $5 Canal Street knockoff she helped acquire
jokes.explanation: Justifies 8+: The dramatic irony is excellent — Bobbi, the supposed bag expert who was arrested for buying knockoffs hours ago, is praising the craftsmanship of a knockoff she participated in obtaining. 'Nice backstitch' as a technical compliment on a $5 bag is delicious. The 'high-class jewess' callback ties back to Ilana's nail salon speech (id 7), adding resonance. Rewatch bonus because you catch all the irony layers immediately.

jokes.text: Elliott explains pegging to the assembled Shiva guests: 'Straight men and gay men alike both enjoy prostate stimulation. And it's very popular with married couples.' / 'Is it?' / 'Probably.'
jokes.setup: The Shiva guests are learning about pegging in real time
jokes.punchline: 'Is it?' / 'Probably.' — Elliott's qualified confidence in his own claim is the joke; he's essentially making this up as he goes
jokes.explanation: 'Probably' after 'Is it very popular with married couples?' is the precise right amount of hedge — Elliott is presenting himself as an authority and immediately undermining himself. The entire Shiva-as-sex-ed scene is great sustained cringe, and Elliott's confident-but-uncertain delivery is character-specific.

jokes.text: Bobbi: 'We were at the Millers' once. It turned out it was a swingers party. No, but we didn't have sex with anybody... but we stayed 'cause the food was incredible.' / Elliott: '...and also because the owner's child had died of an overdose and we wanted to be supportive.' / Bobbi: 'Out of support.'
jokes.setup: Bobbi is contextualizing sexual adventurousness with a Wexler family anecdote
jokes.punchline: Two separate justifications for staying at a swingers party — incredible food AND grief support — neither of which is the expected answer ('we participated'). The 'Out of support' button is the closer.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's three best jokes. The joke has two escalations: first the subversion (they stayed for the food, not for sex), then the dark second subversion (also for grief solidarity over a child's drug overdose). 'Out of support' as the final button on the overdose detail is perfectly timed dark comedy. The Wexler family logic — genuine compassion expressed in absurd form — is consistent and specific. This is great comedy writing.

jokes.text: I never got to say goodbye. / The bags know, mom. They know.
jokes.setup: Bobbi is grief-stricken about losing the confiscated knockoff bags
jokes.punchline: Ilana's solemn 'The bags know, mom. They know.' — treating the bags as sentient beings who received the unsaid goodbye — applied to counterfeit handbags
jokes.explanation: Justifies 8+: This is one of the episode's sharpest jokes. Ilana's 'The bags know' applies the language of unfinished grief — the kind you'd use for a person — to mass-produced counterfeit handbags. The sincerity with which she delivers it is perfect. The economy is nearly flawless: five words ('The bags know, mom') contain an entire comedic worldview. It also works as a structural joke because the episode is ostensibly about a grandmother's death and real grief, and here grief language is applied to bags. The irony is gentle and earned.

jokes.text: Hey, you're blocking the whole fucking sidewalk! / (Both women shouting) Who the hell are you, you disrespectful motherfucker? So walk around us, you patriarchal motherfucker! Who do you think you are, talking to us that way? You move around us, fuck face! I'll fucking murder you, you little bitch! You cross the street!
jokes.setup: A stranger yells at them for blocking the sidewalk
jokes.punchline: Bobbi and Ilana immediately escalate to maximum aggression in perfect unison — 'You'll fucking murder him'? At a Shiva day?
jokes.explanation: 'Patriarchal motherfucker' is a fun coinage for Ilana to use — she's feminist even mid-street-rage. The Bobbi/Ilana unison aggression mirrors their earlier unison moments (bisexual alien, Yes). Slightly overlong but the energy is good. The pivot to 'I love you so much... you are my world' immediately after is the real joke.

jokes.text: Abbi: 'I pegged. Yeah, I pegged, but I kind of did it for Grandma Esther, you know? In a way.'
jokes.setup: Abbi is delivering an emotional speech at the Shiva about living with no regrets
jokes.punchline: The speech culminates in crediting Grandma Esther's legacy as the inspiration for pegging Jeremy
jokes.explanation: The episode's thematic payoff. Abbi's sincere emotional speech about living fully ends with 'I kind of did it for Grandma Esther, you know? In a way.' This is the exact joke the entire episode was building to — the pegging and the legacy of Grandma Esther (who fucked Little Richard) are one and the same. Earned, earned, earned. Scores 8+ because the structure of the entire episode supports this single punchline.

jokes.text: Bobbi's friend cries at Abbi's speech; Bobbi: 'Mom, feel something!' / Bobbi: 'I'm feeling the inside of this bag, okay?'
jokes.setup: Everyone is having an emotional moment at the Shiva
jokes.punchline: Bobbi's version of 'feeling something' is literally palpating the interior of Abbi's knockoff bag
jokes.explanation: Perfect character comedy callback — 'I'm feeling the inside of this bag' as emotional engagement is Bobbi's entire character in one line. The double meaning of 'feeling something' (emotional vs. tactile) is elegant. Scores 8+ because it's economical, character-specific, and a satisfying culmination of the bag obsession arc.

jokes.text: Abbi to the friend who farted: 'I promise I will never, ever tell anybody about your fart. I love you.'
jokes.setup: The opening fart gag (id 60) is still in play
jokes.punchline: Abbi is maintaining the fart secret pact with someone she barely knows, choosing now — at the Shiva emotional peak — to formally renew it
jokes.explanation: Good callback that honors the opening fart gag. The 'I love you' attached to the pact renewal in this emotionally charged setting is a nice absurdist touch.

jokes.text: Elliott: 'Don't forget to wear a condom.' / Bobbi: 'Or not... I don't know what's appropriate in this situation.' / Ilana: 'Listen to me: Get that ass.'
jokes.setup: Abbi is leaving to return to Jeremy
jokes.punchline: The goodbye condom advice (id 14) is reprised by Elliott, then Bobbi — who for once is uncertain about which advice is appropriate — then Ilana cuts through with 'Get that ass'
jokes.explanation: The callback to the condom goodbye now being reprised by the entire Wexler family, with Bobbi's unusual uncertainty ('I don't know what's appropriate in this situation' — because she knows it's a strap-on situation), resolved by Ilana's perfect 'Get that ass.' Three-beat character symphony. Scores 8 because the family has been well-established by this point and the callback landing differently (Bobbi now knows the context) earns its upgrade.

jokes.text: Bobbi has left her phone in one of her many Canal Street bags — which are now all evidence in a police investigation. She starts tearing open the bags: 'Don't put 'em on the filthy floor! Where's the pashmina?'
jokes.setup: Bobbi's phone is vibrating inside one of her confiscated-then-returned bags
jokes.punchline: Bobbi opens the bags with escalating chaos; the pashmina is produced as a floor-protection measure for the knockoff bags on the floor of a funeral
jokes.explanation: The pashmina detail — using a luxury scarf to protect knockoff bags from a dirty floor, at a funeral — is good character comedy. The bag chaos is a satisfying escalation of the bag theme.

jokes.text: Bobbi and Ilana counting aloud: 'One bag, two bag, three bag, four. Bags, bags, bags. Five bags, six bags, seven bags, more.' Both: 'Jesus Christ.'
jokes.setup: The bags keep coming out of the suitcase
jokes.punchline: The counting escalates Dr. Seuss-style, then both women are defeated by the quantity — 'Jesus Christ' said in unison
jokes.explanation: The Dr. Seuss rhyme scheme here lightly echoes the earlier 'Pega-Seuss' wordplay in the same episode, which is a nice ambient connection. The unified 'Jesus Christ' as defeat is a good button.

jokes.text: During arrest, Ilana: 'Guess we're being let off easy 'cause we're extremely white.' / Bobbi: 'Yeah, that's the prison industrial complex for you.' / Officer: 'Want to get back in the car?' / Both: 'We just want to make sure our voices are heard.' / Officer: 'They were... you're very loud.'
jokes.setup: They've been arrested for carrying counterfeit bags; they're loudly complaining
jokes.punchline: They acknowledge their white privilege getting them off easy, cite the prison industrial complex seriously, then immediately transition to asking their voices be heard — the officer's 'you're very loud' is the perfect deflating button
jokes.explanation: Sharp social commentary that doesn't lose its comedy. Ilana's self-aware white privilege acknowledgment followed by Bobbi's 'prison industrial complex' is the Wexler family at its most satirically precise. The officer's 'you're very loud' undercuts the self-congratulatory activism perfectly. Scores 8 for doing genuine ideological work within a tight comedic structure.

jokes.text: Guess we're being let off easy 'cause we're extremely white. / Yeah, that's the prison industrial complex for you.
jokes.setup: They're being released without serious consequences for the counterfeit arrests
jokes.punchline: Ilana and Bobbi casually acknowledge their white privilege as a mundane fact while both benefiting from it — 'prison industrial complex' in Bobbi's mouth is unexpected
jokes.explanation: The show often punctures its own premise with this kind of self-aware social commentary. Ilana's 'extremely white' self-diagnosis and Bobbi's accurate use of 'prison industrial complex' as a suburban Long Island mom is funny because of the class/vocabulary mismatch. Subversive without being preachy.

jokes.text: Officer: 'We can't give you the bags. They're evidence in an ongoing counterfeit investigation.' / Ilana: 'Enjoy your new handbags!' / Bobbi: ''Ongoing'.'
jokes.setup: The police confiscate the knockoff bags as evidence
jokes.punchline: Ilana implies the cops are keeping the bags for themselves; Bobbi sarcastically air-quotes 'ongoing' — suggesting the investigation is pretextual bag theft
jokes.explanation: Bobbi's sarcastic air-quote of 'ongoing' is the better half of this two-joke unit. The conspiracy worldview (from Illuminati to Bloomberg to now this) is consistent.

jokes.text: Bobbi on the Zabar's call from jail: 'Don't you yell at me!'
jokes.setup: Bobbi is using her one phone call to deal with her Zabar's order
jokes.punchline: Zabar's is apparently yelling at Bobbi — suggesting she is a difficult, known customer who has been threatened before
jokes.explanation: The Zabar's argument implying Bobbi has an adversarial history with them is great. Her outrage while using her one arrest call is perfect character logic.

jokes.text: Jeremy retrieves a replacement dildo he bought for Abbi — 'it's not my dildo' — and Abbi's reaction
jokes.setup: Abbi returns to Jeremy's apartment; he has a surprise
jokes.punchline: Jeremy bought a replacement dildo because he melted hers in the dishwasher — a thoughtful boyfriend gesture applied to a strap-on
jokes.explanation: Jeremy applying standard relationship courtesy (replace what you break) to a strap-on is good character comedy. Sets up the knockoff/shinjo argument which is where the real jokes land.

jokes.text: Abbi: 'Mine was a shinjo.' / Jeremy: '$79, I feel it could get the job done.'
jokes.setup: Jeremy presents his replacement dildo; Abbi identifies hers was a shinjo (premium brand)
jokes.punchline: Jeremy's value proposition — '$79, I feel it could get the job done' — is the consumer pragmatist vs. artisanal specialist conflict
jokes.explanation: 'I feel it could get the job done' is Jeremy's entire worldview in one sentence — practical, non-specialist, good-enough. This sets up the episode's thematic collision between knockoffs and authenticity.

jokes.text: Abbi: 'It's a shinjo. And that's like a cheap knockoff.'
jokes.setup: Abbi explains why the replacement won't do
jokes.punchline: The word 'knockoff' — the episode title — is spoken in context of a dildo, completing the show's title-as-double-meaning joke
jokes.explanation: The episode title being delivered as a punchline about a dildo brand — while the whole episode has been about counterfeit handbags — is genuine structural wit. 'Knockoffs' means the bags AND the replacement dildo simultaneously. The thematic coherence earns this a rewatch bonus.

jokes.text: Jeremy: 'You like your stuff to be specialty and curated. I get it.' / Abbi: 'Like what?' / Jeremy: 'Like, the cider beer, the cardamom? You have a kimono area.'
jokes.setup: Jeremy defends his replacement dildo choice by calling Abbi 'particular'
jokes.punchline: 'You have a kimono area' — a single detail that implies Abbi's apartment contains a dedicated zone for kimonos
jokes.explanation: 'You have a kimono area' is the perfect specificity — not 'you own a kimono' but that she has designated square footage for it. The list (cider beer, cardamom, kimono area) as character analysis is well-constructed. 'Kimono area' is independently quotable.

jokes.text: Abbi: 'Bragging about not judging something just means that you think it should be judged.'
jokes.setup: Jeremy says 'I pegged you last night without any judgment' as proof of his open-mindedness
jokes.punchline: Abbi delivers a genuine piece of social insight that turns Jeremy's defense into an indictment
jokes.explanation: Scores 8+ because this is a genuinely sharp social observation that works on its own terms outside the show. 'Bragging about not judging something means you think it should be judged' is the kind of insight that gets screenshotted and shared. It's also plot-relevant, character-consistent for Abbi (she's sharper than she appears), and economically stated. One of the episode's smartest lines.

jokes.text: Abbi: 'I fucked you in the ass the first night that we did anything, okay? I think that's pretty fucking mature. Plus, you're throwing, like, a temper tantrum 'cause you don't have your specialty leather-blown dildo or whatever.'
jokes.setup: Jeremy has called Abbi 'childish'
jokes.punchline: Abbi's rebuttal — citing first-night pegging as evidence of maturity — then mis-describing the shinjo as 'leather-blown'
jokes.explanation: Using first-night pegging as a maturity credential is great Abbi-logic — she has a point, but the framing is absurd. 'Leather-blown' is also a beautiful mistake (she's confusing leather-tanning with glass-blowing), which sets up Jeremy's pedantic correction.

jokes.text: Jeremy: 'Leather is tanned! It's not blown, like glass.' / Abbi: 'Oh, I'm sorry, I'm not a leathersmith.'
jokes.setup: Abbi said 'leather-blown dildo'
jokes.punchline: Jeremy corrects the manufacturing process; Abbi's comeback is 'I'm not a leathersmith' — a word that barely exists, as a defense
jokes.explanation: 'I'm not a leathersmith' as a defensive response is excellent — it's both a non-sequitur and a perfectly logical defense simultaneously. The word 'leathersmith' sounds almost real, which makes it funnier than a clearly made-up word. Scores 8+ for economy and the specific type of comedic coinage.

jokes.text: Abbi: 'The only reason I pegged you in the first place is because Ilana's grandmother died and she fucked Little Richard.'
jokes.setup: The fight is escalating; Abbi is justifying her actions
jokes.punchline: Abbi traces the causal chain back to Grandma Esther's sexual history with Little Richard as the root cause of the pegging
jokes.explanation: The full causal chain — grandmother's death → Little Richard sex → no regrets legacy → pegging Jeremy — delivered as a single sentence is one of the episode's great callbacks. The sentence is logically coherent within the episode's world, which makes it funnier. 'She fucked Little Richard' as explanation for a contemporary sexual act is perfect absurdist comedy with excellent structural payoff. Scores 8+ easily.

jokes.text: Real mature! / Also, a fucking ton of people play that song and love it! / Headphones.
jokes.setup: Abbi has criticized his Alanis Morissette; he's defending it
jokes.punchline: 'Headphones.' — Abbi's one-word closer after his defensive speech is a perfect deadpan dismissal that also makes a practical point
jokes.explanation: Justifies 8+: 'Headphones.' as a one-word closer to a fight is elite comedy economy. It's simultaneously practical advice, a devastating put-down, and an exit line. The contrast between Jeremy's lengthy emotional defense and Abbi's single-word response makes it land hard. Maximum economy score.

jokes.text: You're gonna find someone else to peg. There are infinite holes in the sea.
jokes.setup: Ilana consoling Abbi about losing Jeremy
jokes.punchline: 'There are infinite holes in the sea' — a perfect corruption of 'plenty of fish in the sea' that simultaneously works as a sex joke and as genuine comfort
jokes.explanation: The episode's best joke and one of the best in the series. 'Infinite holes in the sea' is a modification of 'plenty of fish in the sea' that: (1) works perfectly as sexual slang in context, (2) is genuinely comforting in its own weird way, (3) could only come from Ilana, (4) is immediately quotable and has become a show catchphrase. The word 'infinite' instead of 'plenty' is the cherry — it's more generous and more absurd. The structure is flawless: it sounds like it's going somewhere generic then pivots hard. This clears the 8+ bar by a significant margin.

jokes.text: Maybe even, I don't know... Girl holes? / I'm not pegging you, dude. / You gotta stop bringing it up.
jokes.setup: Ilana has been trying to get Abbi to peg her for the whole series
jokes.punchline: Ilana floats the possibility with the casual 'I don't know' hedge; Abbi's 'You gotta stop bringing it up' implies this is a recurring pitch
jokes.explanation: Good running gag moment. 'Girl holes' as a gentle euphemism from the usually explicit Ilana is funny in its relative restraint. 'You gotta stop bringing it up' confirms the pattern without being exhausted by it.

jokes.text: Oh, and also, I'm back on [censored]. I like them again.
jokes.setup: Random end-of-conversation declaration
jokes.punchline: The bleep implies Ilana is back on something whose name is bleeped — drugs, a person, a sex act — delivered as casual life-update
jokes.explanation: The bleep-as-mystery-substance joke is a reliable sitcom device but works here because of Ilana's complete casualness. The 'I like them again' implies a complex prior relationship with whatever it is. Functional absurdism.

jokes.text: Forgot about precious asshole. / But my asshole is fair game, of course!
jokes.setup: Elliot has expressed uncertainty about his own asshole
jokes.punchline: Bobbi's 'Forgot about precious asshole' as mock-sorrow, then Elliot's immediate 'But my asshole is fair game, of course!' — he's drawing a one-way boundary
jokes.explanation: 'Precious asshole' as a phrase Bobbi adopts sarcastically is very funny. Elliot's immediate offer that his own asshole is 'fair game, of course!' establishes his actual position — he wants to receive, not give. The 'of course' implies he considers this obvious. Strong callback extension.
